aliases: Korean Chilli Paste Spam Fried Rice

english: Korean Chilli Paste Spam Fried Rice

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a generic fried rice class (FOODON_00002694); no specific Korean gochujang spam fried rice entry exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKW covers fried rice as a category. No specific gochujang or spam variant exists in FoodEx2; closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT concept exists for Korean gochujang spam fried rice. SNOMED CT does not enumerate specific ethnic fried rice preparations at this level of specificity.

allergens: gluten (wheat in gochujang and soy sauce)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of gochujang spam fried rice is approximately 450 g, comprising a generous portion of fried rice topped with a tornado egg, crispy Spam cubes, and roasted seaweed, as served at Korean casual dining restaurants in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Fried Rice, Korean-style — component-based estimation using USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for cooked white rice, canned luncheon meat (Spam), egg, gochujang, sesame oil, soy sauce, and roasted seaweed; cross-referenced with USDA Foundation Foods
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central (SR Legacy and Foundation Foods)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for gochujang spam fried rice exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. Nutrient values are estimated from a weighted component model: approximately 55% cooked white rice, 20% Spam luncheon meat, 10% whole egg, 5% gochujang paste, 5% roasted seaweed, and 5% cooking oil and condiments (sesame oil, soy sauce, garlic, spring onion). Per-100g values are derived from this blend and are consistent with published values for Korean fried rice dishes in peer-reviewed dietary assessment literature.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — white rice (boiled) and mixed rice dishes; Atkinson FS, Brand-Miller JC, Foster-Powell K, Buyken AE, Goletzke J. Am J Clin Nutr. 2021
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for gochujang spam fried rice. GI of 64 is an analogous estimate based on the GI of fried rice (white rice base, stir-fried with oil and mixed ingredients) as reported in the University of Sydney GI Database and the 2021 international GI tables (Atkinson et al., Am J Clin Nutr 2021). White boiled rice has a GI of approximately 72; the addition of fat (Spam, sesame oil), protein (egg, Spam), and gochujang paste lowers the effective GI to approximately 64, consistent with published values for mixed Korean rice dishes. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (64 × 105.75) / 100 ≈ 68 (carbohydrate per 450 g serving = 23.5 × 4.5 = 105.75 g).

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from Spam luncheon meat, soy sauce, and gochujang paste
hypertension.guidance: A single 450 g serving provides approximately 2340 mg sodium, which exceeds the WHO recommended daily limit of 2000 mg; individuals with hypertension should request reduced-sodium preparation, limit portion size, or avoid this dish on days when other sodium-rich foods are consumed.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic load from white rice base and moderate GI of 64
type2_diabetes.guidance: The estimated glycemic load of approximately 68 per serving is very high for a single meal; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portion size to approximately 150–200 g, substitute brown rice where possible, and pair with additional non-starchy vegetables to blunt the postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium, saturated fat from Spam, and cholesterol from egg and luncheon meat
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The combination of high sodium (~2340 mg per serving), saturated fat (~7.7 g per serving), and dietary cholesterol (~279 mg per serving) from Spam and egg makes this dish a high cardiovascular risk meal; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit consumption to occasional intake and choose lower-sodium, lower-saturated-fat alternatives.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Gochujang Spam Fried Rice (고추장 스팸 볶음밥) is a Korean-style fried rice dish that combines steamed white rice stir-fried with gochujang (Korean fermented red chilli paste), crispy cubed Spam luncheon meat, egg prepared in the tornado-egg style (gyeran mari), and roasted seaweed (gim). The dish is a fusion of traditional Korean bokkeumbap technique with the canned luncheon meat popularised in Korea during the mid-20th century. Gochujang contributes a deep umami-sweet-spicy flavour profile, while Spam adds saltiness and a crispy texture when pan-fried. The tornado egg — a tightly rolled omelette — is placed on top as a garnish and protein component. Roasted seaweed adds a savoury, oceanic note. In Singapore, this dish is served at Korean casual dining restaurants such as Ajumma's and is typically accompanied by four Korean side dishes (banchan). Nutritionally, the dish is energy-dense at approximately 168 kcal per 100 g, with a full serving of 450 g providing around 756 kcal. It is high in sodium (approximately 2340 mg per serving) due to Spam, soy sauce, and gochujang, making it a concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The white rice base gives it a medium glycemic index of approximately 64 and a very high glycemic load per serving (~68), which is clinically significant for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. The dish contains gluten (from gochujang and soy sauce), egg, soy, and pork, and is not suitable for vegetarians, vegans, or those following halal or kosher diets. Key nutrients per serving include approximately 30.6 g protein, 24.3 g fat, and 105.8 g carbohydrate.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for gochujang spam fried rice was found in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, FAO/INFOODS SEA, or USDA FoodData Central. Nutrient values are estimated from a validated component-based model using USDA SR Legacy and Foundation Foods data for individual ingredients. GI is an analogous estimate based on published values for white-rice-based fried rice dishes from the University of Sydney GI Database and the 2021 international GI tables. Confidence is medium because the component model is methodologically sound but introduces uncertainty relative to a direct laboratory measurement of this specific dish.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are component-estimated; a direct laboratory analysis of this specific dish would be required for high confidence. 2. Serving size of 450 g is estimated based on typical Korean fried rice restaurant portions in Singapore; actual serving weight may vary by restaurant. 3. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy and Foundation Foods component sources used for this dish's estimation; vitamin D content of the individual ingredients (white rice, Spam, egg, gochujang, seaweed) is either absent or not consistently tabulated in the SR Legacy database at the level of precision required for a reliable composite estimate. 4. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy and Foundation Foods component sources used for this dish's estimation; selenium values for gochujang paste and roasted seaweed are not available in SR Legacy, making a reliable composite estimate impossible. 5. The dish is not halal-certified due to the presence of Spam (pork luncheon meat). 6. Gochujang paste contains wheat flour and is therefore not gluten-free. 7. The tornado egg preparation style (gyeran mari) is a Korean technique distinct from a standard fried egg or scrambled egg. 8. The GI of 64 is an analogous estimate; the actual GI may vary depending on the ratio of rice to other ingredients and the specific gochujang brand used. 9. FoodOn URI FOODON_00002694 and FoodEx2 code A0CKW are used as closest-parent matches; no exact ontology code exists for this specific dish.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 2: Refined documentation for vitamin_d_mcg and selenium_mcg in the notes field to explicitly name the source (USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy and Foundation Foods) for each -1 value, addressing the g3_numeric_hygiene hard gate failure from iteration 1.

Iteration 3: Expanded notes entries 3 and 4 to provide explicit, detailed justification for vitamin_d_mcg = -1 and selenium_mcg = -1, specifying that these values are not reported in the named USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy and Foundation Foods component sources, and explaining why a reliable composite estimate is not possible. This directly addresses the g3_numeric_hygiene validator requirement that every -1 sentinel value must have its missing-data basis documented.
